title: Data Link: Ethernet, Framing, MAC & Switching

lessons.title: The Data Link Layer: Frames and MAC Addresses
lessons.content:
## Getting data to the device next door

You already know the physical layer just pushes raw bits (1s and 0s) down a wire or through the air. But a stream of bare bits is not very useful on its own. Which bits belong together? Who is this data for? Did any bits get garbled along the way?

The **data link layer** answers those questions. It sits **just above** the physical layer, and its job is simple to state: **move data reliably between two devices on the same local network** — for example, your laptop and the Wi-Fi router sitting in your house. "Local network" just means the devices near you that are directly connected, like everything in your home or one office.

## Frames and addresses

The data link layer takes the bits and groups them into **frames**. A **frame** is a chunk of data with a clear *start*, some *address labels*, the actual *payload* (the data being carried), and an *error-check* value at the end. Think of it like an envelope: it has a front (who it's for, who it's from), the letter inside, and a tamper-check seal.

To write the address on that envelope, every network device uses a **MAC address** (Media Access Control address). A MAC address is a **permanent hardware ID burned into the network chip at the factory**. It is **48 bits** long and is written as six pairs of hex digits, like `a1:b2:c3:44:55:66`. Because it is built into the hardware, it normally never changes, and it identifies exactly **one device on the local network**.

So a frame says, in effect: "From MAC `aa:aa:…`, to MAC `bb:bb:…`, here is the data." The address makes sure the frame reaches the one nearby device it is meant for, the same way an apartment number gets a letter to the right resident in your building.

## MAC vs IP — a quick heads-up

You may have heard of **IP addresses** too. Here is the key difference, which the next sub-topics will build on: a **MAC address is the local, permanent hardware address** used to reach a device on the *same* network, while an **IP address is a logical address** used to find devices *across* different networks. For now, just remember: MAC = local and permanent; IP = logical and used to travel between networks.

## Recap

The data link layer turns raw bits into addressed **frames** and delivers them between two devices on the **same local network**, using permanent **MAC addresses** to label who each frame is for.

items.prompt: Study the diagram comparing a MAC address and an IP address. Based on it, which statement is correct?
choices: A MAC address changes every time you move your device to a new network
A MAC address is a permanent, local hardware ID, while an IP address is a logical address used to cross networks
An IP address is burned into the chip and a MAC address is assigned by software
MAC and IP addresses are two names for the exact same thing
items.explanation: As the diagram shows, the MAC address is the permanent, factory-burned hardware ID that identifies a device on the local network, while the IP address is a logical (software-assigned) address used to reach devices across different networks. They do different jobs at different layers.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what is the main job of the data link layer?
choices: To choose the best path for data across the whole Internet
To move data reliably between two devices on the same local network
To convert text into the radio waves used by Wi-Fi
To decide which website a browser should open
items.explanation: The data link layer sits just above the physical layer and handles delivery between two directly connected devices on the same local network (such as your laptop and your home router). Choosing paths across many networks is the job of a higher layer (the network layer, using IP).

items.prompt: What is a 'frame' in networking?
choices: A single bit travelling down a wire
A chunk of data with a start, address labels, a payload, and an error-check at the end
The metal case that holds a network switch
A list of every website a device has visited
items.explanation: A frame is the data link layer's 'envelope': it groups bits into one unit with a clear start, address labels (who it is for and from), the payload (the data being carried), and an error-check value at the end. A single bit is just part of the physical layer's bit stream.

items.prompt: How long is a MAC address, and how is it usually written?
choices: 32 bits, written as four numbers separated by dots like 192.168.1.5
48 bits, written as six pairs of hex digits like a1:b2:c3:44:55:66
8 bits, written as a single number from 0 to 255
128 bits, written as eight groups separated by colons
items.explanation: A MAC address is 48 bits long and is written as six pairs of hexadecimal digits, for example a1:b2:c3:44:55:66. The dotted-decimal form like 192.168.1.5 is an IPv4 address, and the 128-bit colon form is IPv6 — both are different from a MAC address.

items.prompt: Where does a device's MAC address come from, and how often does it normally change?
choices: It is typed in by the user and changes every day
It is burned into the network chip at the factory and normally never changes
It is handed out fresh by the Wi-Fi router every time the device connects
It is calculated from the current time, so it is always different
items.explanation: A MAC address is a permanent hardware ID burned into the network chip when it is manufactured, so it normally stays the same for the life of the device. The address that a router hands out when you connect is the IP address, which is a different, logical address.

items.prompt: A MAC address vs an IP address — what is the key difference?
choices: A MAC address is the local, permanent hardware address; an IP address is a logical address used to cross between networks
A MAC address is used on the Internet; an IP address only works inside your house
A MAC address is longer than an IP address and is always written in decimal
There is no difference; both are assigned by the factory and never change
items.explanation: This is one of the most important distinctions in networking. The MAC address is the permanent, hardware-level address that identifies a device on the *local* network. The IP address is a logical, software-assigned address used to reach devices *across* different networks. Data crossing the Internet relies on IP; delivery to the device right next to you relies on MAC.

items.prompt: Why does a frame need to carry a destination MAC address at all?
choices: So the data link layer can deliver the frame to the correct nearby device instead of just every device
So the frame can be encrypted before it is sent
So the receiver knows which website the data came from
So the physical layer can decide what voltage to use
items.explanation: The destination MAC is the address label on the envelope: it tells the local network which specific nearby device the frame is meant for, just as an apartment number directs a letter to one resident. Without it, there would be no way to single out the intended device. Encryption and website identity are handled by higher layers.

items.prompt: A good real-world analogy for a MAC address is:
choices: The temporary ticket number you get at a deli counter
A resident's permanent ID that an envelope is addressed to so it reaches the right apartment
The speed limit posted on a highway
The colour of the envelope the letter is mailed in
items.explanation: A MAC address is like a resident's permanent ID used to address an envelope to one specific apartment in a building — it is fixed and identifies exactly one recipient on the local network. A deli ticket changes each visit, which is more like a temporary IP address than a permanent MAC.

items.prompt: Which layer does the data link layer sit directly above, and what does it receive from it?
choices: The application layer; it receives finished web pages
The physical layer; it receives a raw stream of bits
The transport layer; it receives reliable connections
There is no layer below it; the data link layer is the lowest
items.explanation: The data link layer sits just above the physical layer. The physical layer moves raw bits (1s and 0s) over the wire or air, and the data link layer organizes those bits into addressed frames. The physical layer is the lowest layer, not the data link layer.

items.prompt: Two laptops are connected to the same home Wi-Fi router. At the data link layer, how does one laptop's frame reach the other?
choices: It is addressed using the other laptop's permanent MAC address on the local network
It must travel across the Internet and come back
It is sent to every device in the entire city until the right one answers
It uses the website's domain name as the address
items.explanation: Both laptops are on the same local network, so the data link layer delivers the frame directly using the destination laptop's MAC address. There is no need to leave the local network. Domain names and crossing the Internet involve higher layers, not local frame delivery.

lessons.title: Inside an Ethernet Frame, Field by Field
lessons.content:
## What is Ethernet?

**Ethernet** is the dominant set of rules for **wired local networks**. When you plug a cable into a wall jack or into the back of a router, you are almost certainly using Ethernet. It defines exactly how a frame is laid out so that any Ethernet device can understand any other.

## The envelope, laid out left to right

Let's open up one Ethernet frame and read it from start to finish. Picture it as a row of labeled boxes:

1. **Preamble + Start delimiter** — a short "wake-up" pattern that lets the receiver's clock lock onto the incoming bits. It is *not* counted as part of the frame and is *not* error-checked. Think of it as the receiver clearing its throat before the real message.
2. **Destination MAC (6 bytes)** — who the frame is **for**.
3. **Source MAC (6 bytes)** — who the frame is **from**.
4. **Type (2 bytes)** — what kind of data is inside, so the receiver knows how to read it (for example, a value meaning "this carries an IP packet").
5. **Payload (46 to 1500 bytes)** — the actual data being carried.
6. **FCS (4 bytes)** — the **Frame Check Sequence**, the error-check at the very end.

## What the FCS is for

Wires and radios are not perfect; a bit can flip on the way. The **FCS is an error-check value** the sender calculates from the rest of the frame using a math formula called a **CRC** (Cyclic Redundancy Check — you don't need the math, just the idea). The receiver runs the same calculation. If its answer matches the FCS, the frame is almost certainly intact. **If it does not match, the frame is corrupt and is thrown away.**

Here is the key limit: Ethernet **detects** errors but does **not fix** them. A bad frame is simply discarded. Getting the data resent (if anyone bothers) is left to a higher layer. So the FCS is a smoke detector, not a fire department: it tells you something is wrong, but it does not repair the damage.

## Recap

An Ethernet frame is a labeled envelope: destination MAC, source MAC, a type field, the payload, and an FCS error-check at the end. The FCS lets the receiver detect a corrupted frame and discard it — Ethernet catches errors, it does not correct them.

items.prompt: Look at the labeled Ethernet frame diagram. Reading left to right, which order are the main fields in?
choices: FCS, payload, type, source MAC, destination MAC
Destination MAC, source MAC, type, payload, FCS
Payload, destination MAC, FCS, type, source MAC
Type, payload, destination MAC, source MAC, FCS
items.explanation: As the diagram lays out, after the wake-up preamble the frame goes: destination MAC (who it's for), source MAC (who it's from), type (what's inside), payload (the data), and finally the FCS error-check at the end.

items.prompt: What is Ethernet, in plain terms?
choices: The single cable that connects your house to the Internet provider
The dominant set of rules for wired local networks
A brand of Wi-Fi router
The program that opens web pages
items.explanation: Ethernet is the dominant standard (set of rules) for wired local networks — it defines how frames are formatted and sent over cables so any Ethernet device understands any other. It is not a single cable, a brand, or an application.

items.prompt: Within the frame, what do the destination MAC and source MAC fields tell you?
choices: The size of the payload and the speed of the link
Who the frame is for (destination) and who it is from (source)
Which website sent the data and which one will receive it
The error-check value and the wake-up pattern
items.explanation: The destination MAC says which device the frame is for, and the source MAC says which device it came from — the 'to' and 'from' on the envelope. Sizes, speeds, and the error-check are separate concerns, and websites are identified far higher up the stack.

items.prompt: What does the 'type' field in an Ethernet frame do?
choices: It tells the receiver what kind of data is inside, so it knows how to read the payload
It sets how many volts the wire should use
It counts how many devices are on the network
It stores the sender's permanent MAC address
items.explanation: The type field identifies what the payload contains (for example, a value meaning the payload is an IP packet), so the receiver hands the data to the right software. Voltages are a physical-layer concern, and the sender's address lives in the source MAC field.

items.prompt: What is the FCS (Frame Check Sequence) used for?
choices: To encrypt the payload so no one can read it
To let the receiver detect whether the frame was corrupted in transit
To store the destination MAC address
To speed up the connection
items.explanation: The FCS is an error-check value at the end of the frame. The sender computes it from the rest of the frame; the receiver recomputes it and compares. A mismatch means the frame was damaged. The FCS does not encrypt, address, or speed up anything — it only detects errors.

items.prompt: A frame arrives and the receiver's recomputed check does NOT match the FCS. What happens?
choices: Ethernet repairs the damaged bits using the FCS and keeps the frame
The frame is thrown away; Ethernet detects errors but does not fix them
The frame is sent back to the sender at the data link layer for correction
The receiver ignores the FCS and uses the frame anyway
items.explanation: A mismatch means the frame is corrupt, so Ethernet simply discards it. Ethernet's FCS only *detects* errors — it cannot correct them, and Ethernet does not automatically resend the frame itself. Any recovery is left to a higher layer.

items.prompt: Why are the preamble and start delimiter NOT counted as part of the frame or covered by the FCS?
choices: They are just a wake-up timing pattern that helps the receiver lock onto the bits, not real data
They are encrypted separately for security
They contain the most important data, so they get their own check
They are added by the user, not the hardware
items.explanation: The preamble and start delimiter are a fixed 'wake-up' pattern that lets the receiver's clock synchronize and find where the frame begins. They carry no message data, so they sit outside the frame proper and outside the FCS calculation.

items.prompt: A good analogy for the FCS is:
choices: A spell-checker that quietly fixes your typos for you
A smoke detector — it tells you something is wrong but does not put out the fire
A return address so the letter can be mailed back
A stamp that pays for delivery
items.explanation: The FCS is like a smoke detector: it alerts the receiver that the frame is damaged, but it does not repair anything. A spell-checker that auto-corrects would be error *correction*, which Ethernet's FCS does not do.

items.prompt: The payload of a standard Ethernet frame can hold roughly how much data?
choices: Exactly 1 byte, always
Up to about 1500 bytes
Up to about 1 million bytes
There is no limit at all
items.explanation: A standard Ethernet payload holds up to about 1500 bytes (with a small minimum so frames are not too short). This 1500-byte ceiling is the usual maximum amount of data per frame; bigger messages are split across many frames.

items.prompt: If a frame's data gets corrupted and dropped, whose responsibility is it to recover that lost data?
choices: Ethernet automatically resends every dropped frame itself
A higher layer must handle any resending; Ethernet just discards the bad frame
The preamble re-sends the frame
Nothing can ever recover it under any circumstances
items.explanation: Ethernet's job ends at detecting and discarding the corrupt frame. Whether the data is resent is decided by a higher layer (you will meet protocols that do this later). So Ethernet does not recover the data itself, but recovery is still possible above it.

lessons.title: Switches, Hubs, and How a Switch Learns
lessons.content:
## The box in the middle

In a real local network the devices are not wired directly to each other — they all plug into a box in the middle. The smart version of that box is a **switch**. A **switch** is a device with many ports (sockets) that **learns which device is on which port** and forwards each frame **only where it needs to go**.

Think of a smart mailroom. At first the mail clerk doesn't know where anyone sits. But every time a person hands in outgoing mail, the clerk notes their name and desk. Soon the clerk has a list and can carry each letter straight to the right desk instead of walking the whole floor.

## How a switch learns: MAC → port

The switch does exactly that, using MAC addresses. It keeps a **MAC address table** — a simple list of *which MAC address is reachable through which port*.

- When a frame **comes in** on a port, the switch reads the **source MAC** and records: "this MAC lives on this port." That is how it *learns*.
- When it needs to **send** a frame, it looks up the **destination MAC** in the table. If it knows the port, it forwards the frame out **only that one port**. If it has not learned that address yet, it sends the frame out **all** ports (just for that frame) to find the device.

So a switch **learns from the source address and forwards by the destination address**. Over a few moments it builds a full map of the network and cuts out wasted traffic.

## A hub: the dumb version

Before switches were cheap, networks used a **hub**. A hub is a dumb version of the same box: it does **no learning**. Every frame that comes in is **copied out to every other port**, whether or not the destination is there. That is wasteful and not private — every device sees every frame. A switch sends a frame only to the right port; a hub floods it everywhere.

## A bit of history: collisions and CSMA/CD

Old networks (and hubs) shared one wire, so only **one device could 'talk' at a time**. If two transmitted at once, their signals overlapped — a **collision** — and both had to stop and retry. The rulebook that managed this was called **CSMA/CD**: roughly, *listen first, and if you hear a collision, back off and try again later.*

Switches mostly made this history. Because a switch gives each device its own dedicated connection, devices can send and receive at the same time without colliding. So today CSMA/CD is mostly a story about how things used to work — worth knowing, but not something a modern switched network relies on.

## Recap

A **switch** learns which MAC address is on which port (learning from the source, forwarding by the destination) and sends each frame only where it belongs. A **hub** is the dumb older box that floods every frame to every port. Collisions and CSMA/CD were how shared-wire networks coped before switches made them mostly obsolete.

items.prompt: Compare the switch and hub in the diagram. What is the key difference shown?
choices: The switch floods the frame to every device; the hub sends it to only one
The switch forwards the frame to only the correct port; the hub copies it to every port
Both send the frame to every device equally
The hub learns MAC addresses and the switch does not
items.explanation: The diagram shows the switch sending the frame down only the one line that leads to the right device, using its learned MAC table, while the hub copies the same frame out to all of its ports. The switch is selective; the hub is not.

items.prompt: What does a switch do?
choices: It picks the best route for data across the whole Internet
It learns which device is on which port and forwards each frame only where it needs to go
It copies every frame to every port without thinking
It converts Wi-Fi signals into electricity
items.explanation: A switch is the smart box in the middle of a local network: it learns which MAC address is reachable on which port and forwards each frame only out the correct port. Copying every frame to every port is what a (dumb) hub does, and routing across the Internet is a higher-layer job.

items.prompt: How does a switch LEARN which device is on which port?
choices: It reads the source MAC of each incoming frame and records that MAC against the port it arrived on
It asks every device to type in its location
It reads the destination MAC and guesses the port
It is pre-programmed at the factory with every device's location
items.explanation: When a frame arrives, the switch looks at its source MAC (who sent it) and notes 'this MAC is on this port.' That builds the MAC address table over time. It forwards using the destination MAC, but it learns from the source MAC.

items.prompt: When a switch needs to send a frame and it already knows which port the destination MAC is on, what does it do?
choices: It floods the frame out of every port to be safe
It forwards the frame out only that one port
It drops the frame because it already knows the device
It sends the frame back out the port it came in on
items.explanation: If the destination MAC is in its table, the switch sends the frame out only the single port that leads to that device — efficient and private. Flooding to all ports only happens when the switch has *not* yet learned where the destination is.

items.prompt: What is the main difference between a hub and a switch?
choices: A hub learns MAC addresses; a switch does not
A hub copies every frame to every port; a switch forwards a frame only to the correct port
A hub is faster and smarter than a switch
A hub works only with Wi-Fi and a switch only with cables
items.explanation: A hub is the dumb box: it floods every incoming frame out all other ports with no learning. A switch learns where each device is and forwards each frame only to the right port. That makes switches faster, more private, and the modern default.

items.prompt: A switch receives a frame for a destination MAC it has NOT learned yet. What does it do?
choices: It throws the frame away because it doesn't know the device
It sends the frame out all ports (except the one it arrived on) to reach the device
It keeps the frame forever until the device sends something
It rewrites the destination MAC to one it knows
items.explanation: When the destination is unknown, the switch errs on the side of delivery: it floods the frame out every port except the ingress port. Whichever device is the real destination will respond, and the switch will then learn its port for next time.

items.prompt: The 'smart mailroom that learns everyone's desk' is an analogy for which device?
choices: A hub
A switch
A single network cable
A MAC address
items.explanation: The smart mailroom that notes each person's desk and then delivers letters straight to the right place is a switch — it learns which MAC is on which port and forwards accordingly. A hub would be a clerk who drops a copy of every letter on every desk.

items.prompt: What was a 'collision' on an old shared-wire network?
choices: Two devices transmitting at the same time so their signals overlapped and both had to retry
Two cables physically tangling together
A frame arriving with a bad FCS
A switch forwarding to the wrong port
items.explanation: On a shared wire only one device could transmit at a time. If two sent at once their signals overlapped — a collision — and both stopped and retried. This is unrelated to cables tangling, FCS errors, or switch forwarding mistakes.

items.prompt: Why is CSMA/CD mostly just history on modern networks?
choices: Because Wi-Fi made all cables obsolete
Because switches give each device its own connection, so devices can send and receive at once without colliding
Because frames no longer have a destination MAC
Because CSMA/CD was made illegal
items.explanation: CSMA/CD existed to manage collisions on a shared wire. A switch gives each device a dedicated link, so devices can transmit and receive simultaneously and collisions essentially disappear. CSMA/CD is still worth knowing as history, but switched networks don't depend on it.

items.prompt: In one phrase, a switch 'learns from the source and forwards by the destination.' What does that mean?
choices: It records where frames come FROM, but decides where to send them based on who they are FOR
It ignores both the source and destination addresses
It learns the destination and forwards by the source
It only works if every frame is a broadcast
items.explanation: A switch reads the *source* MAC of arriving frames to learn which device sits on which port, and it reads the *destination* MAC to decide which port to forward a frame out of. Source teaches it the map; destination tells it where this frame should go.

lessons.title: Broadcast vs Unicast, VLANs & Frame Size
lessons.content:
## Three ways to address a frame

A frame's destination MAC can aim at different numbers of devices. There are three flavours:

- **Unicast** — to **one** specific device. This is the everyday case: "this frame is for that laptop."
- **Broadcast** — to **every** device on the local network at once. There is a special all-devices address (every bit set to 1) that means "everyone here, listen up." It is like an announcement over a building's loudspeaker. Switches send a broadcast frame out all ports.
- **Multicast** — to a **specific group** of interested devices, not everyone and not just one. Like a memo sent only to the people who signed up for a particular newsletter.

Knowing these helps explain switch behaviour: a unicast goes to one port (once learned), while a broadcast is flooded to all ports because it is meant for everyone.

## Splitting one switch into VLANs

By default, every device plugged into one switch is on the same local network, and a broadcast reaches all of them. Sometimes you don't want that — say you want the staff computers separated from the guest Wi-Fi for security, even though they share one switch.

A **VLAN** (Virtual LAN, where LAN = Local Area Network) lets you **split one physical switch into separate virtual networks**. Ports in VLAN A behave as one local network; ports in VLAN B behave as a totally separate one. A broadcast in VLAN A never reaches VLAN B. To send data between two VLANs you need a **router** (the device that moves data between *different* networks — covered soon).

When VLANs need to span more than one switch, the link between the switches carries traffic for several VLANs at once. That shared link is called a **trunk**, and each frame on it gets a small **VLAN tag** added so the other switch knows which VLAN it belongs to. A normal port connected to a single device (in one VLAN) is called an **access port**.

## MTU and jumbo frames (brief)

Recall the payload tops out around 1500 bytes. That maximum is called the **MTU** (Maximum Transmission Unit) — the largest payload a frame may carry. Some specialized networks (like data centers) raise this to about 9000 bytes using **jumbo frames** to move bulk data more efficiently. The catch: **every device along the path must agree** on the larger size, or the oversized frames get dropped. For everyday networks, 1500 is the norm.

## Recap

Frames can be **unicast** (one device), **broadcast** (everyone), or **multicast** (a group). **VLANs** carve one physical switch into separate virtual networks that need a router to talk between them, with **trunks** and **VLAN tags** carrying multiple VLANs between switches. The **MTU** caps payload size at about 1500 bytes, which **jumbo frames** can raise to ~9000 when every device agrees.

items.prompt: In the VLAN diagram, two VLANs share one physical switch. What does the diagram show about them?
choices: Every device can freely talk to every other device
Devices within the same VLAN can talk to each other, but crossing between VLAN A and VLAN B needs a router
The two VLANs require two separate physical switches
VLANs make the switch forget all MAC addresses
items.explanation: The diagram shows one physical switch split into VLAN A and VLAN B. Devices inside the same VLAN communicate normally, but the two VLANs act as separate networks — crossing between them requires a router, even though they share one switch.

items.prompt: What does a UNICAST frame target?
choices: One specific device
Every device on the local network
A group of devices that signed up
No devices at all
items.explanation: Unicast means the frame is addressed to one specific device — the everyday case. 'Every device' is broadcast, and 'a group that signed up' is multicast.

items.prompt: What is a BROADCAST frame, and what does a switch do with it?
choices: A frame for one device; the switch sends it to one port
A frame for every device on the local network; the switch floods it out all ports
A frame with no destination; the switch drops it
A frame for a device on another network; the switch routes it
items.explanation: A broadcast uses the special all-devices address (every bit set to 1) and is meant for every device on the local network, so a switch floods it out all of its ports. It is like a loudspeaker announcement to the whole building.

items.prompt: How is MULTICAST different from broadcast?
choices: Multicast goes to a specific group of interested devices, not to absolutely everyone
Multicast goes to exactly one device
Multicast goes to devices on other networks only
There is no difference between multicast and broadcast
items.explanation: Multicast targets a particular group of devices that are interested (like a newsletter sent only to subscribers), whereas broadcast hits every device on the local network. Multicast is the in-between option: more than one device, but not all of them.

items.prompt: What is a VLAN?
choices: A faster type of network cable
A way to split one physical switch into separate virtual networks
A permanent hardware address like a MAC
The error-check at the end of a frame
items.explanation: A VLAN (Virtual LAN) divides one physical switch into multiple separate virtual networks. Ports in one VLAN behave as their own local network, isolated from the others, which is useful for separating, say, staff and guest traffic on the same hardware.

items.prompt: Two devices are on the same switch but in different VLANs. How can they communicate?
choices: They cannot communicate under any circumstances
Their traffic must go through a router, which moves data between different networks
They communicate directly because they share one switch
They must first be given the same MAC address
items.explanation: Different VLANs act as separate networks, so frames cannot pass directly between them even on one switch. A router (the device that moves data between different networks) is needed to bridge the two VLANs. Sharing a switch does not by itself let different VLANs talk.

items.prompt: What is a 'trunk' link, and why does it add a VLAN tag to frames?
choices: A link to a single device; the tag stores its MAC
A link between switches that carries several VLANs at once; the tag marks which VLAN each frame belongs to
A backup cable used only when the main one fails
A link to the Internet; the tag stores the website name
items.explanation: A trunk is a link (typically between switches) that carries traffic for multiple VLANs simultaneously. Because several VLANs share the link, each frame gets a small VLAN tag so the receiving switch knows which VLAN it belongs to. An access port, by contrast, connects a single device in one VLAN.

items.prompt: What does MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) mean for an Ethernet frame?
choices: The maximum number of devices on the network
The largest payload a frame may carry, normally about 1500 bytes
The minimum size a frame must be
The speed of the network link
items.explanation: The MTU is the largest payload that fits in one frame — normally about 1500 bytes on standard Ethernet. It is a size limit on the data per frame, not a device count, a minimum size, or a speed.

items.prompt: What is a jumbo frame, and what is the catch?
choices: A frame with no payload; the catch is it carries no data
A frame with a payload up to about 9000 bytes; the catch is every device on the path must agree on the larger size
A frame sent twice for safety; the catch is it doubles traffic
A frame with extra MAC addresses; the catch is it confuses switches
items.explanation: A jumbo frame raises the payload to roughly 9000 bytes to move bulk data more efficiently (common in data centers). The catch is that every device along the path must be set up for the larger size, or the oversized frames are simply dropped.

items.prompt: Why might someone put staff computers and guest Wi-Fi on different VLANs even though they share one switch?
choices: To keep the two groups separated for security, so guests can't reach the staff network
To make the switch run faster for everyone
To give every device the same IP address
To remove the need for any router on the network
items.explanation: VLANs isolate groups of ports into separate virtual networks, so traffic (including broadcasts) in one VLAN does not reach the other. Separating staff and guest traffic for security is a classic use. It does not magically speed up the switch, share IP addresses, or remove the need for a router — in fact a router is needed if the VLANs must talk.
